settings.heading: Open 10 GB JSON, XML & CSV in a blink.
settings.subheading: A zero-copy, memory-mapped viewer that opens, navigates and regex-searches enormous data files without ever loading them into memory.
settings.meta: Free edition available · No file leaves your machine

settings.title: Open the file
settings.body: Drag in a JSON, NDJSON, XML, CSV or TSV file up to ~10 GB — or paste from the clipboard or fetch a URL with auth. It opens instantly, no progress bars.

settings.title: Navigate the tree
settings.body: Everything renders as one uniform, virtualized tree. Jump to any path, bookmark nodes, and read collapsed counts like “products : […], 31 items”.

settings.title: Search & export
settings.body: Regex find, live filter, or JSONPath / XPath queries backed by parallel native search. Then export or convert to JSON, XML or CSV.

settings.label: Files open & search, no in-memory copy
Index per node — ~3 GB for 130M nodes

settings.title: Virtualized tree view
settings.body: Objects, arrays, XML elements and CSV records render in one uniform, collapsible tree. Cost per frame tracks visible rows (~40), never file size.

settings.title: Regex find, instantly
settings.body: Literal or regex search scoped to keys, values or attributes — chip-highlighted matches with ▲▼ stepping, backed by parallel native search.

settings.title: JSONPath & XPath queries
settings.body: Query JSON/CSV with $.store.items[*].name and XML with //item/@id. Combine paths with | to pull several fields at once; results filter the tree.

settings.title: On-demand (lazy) indexing
settings.body: Files too large to index eagerly open anyway — only the paths you expand are materialized, so resident memory tracks the subtree you view.

settings.title: CSV spreadsheet view
settings.body: CSV/TSV opens in a grid with header detection. Leading-zero cells like ZIP codes deliberately stay strings — no silent data loss.

settings.title: Follow tail (tail -f)
settings.body: Follow a growing JSON / NDJSON / log file. Only newly appended lines are parsed each tick, so it stays cheap on huge, live files.

settings.title: Convert & export
settings.body: Export the whole document or any node as pretty JSON, XML or CSV — cross-format conversion built in, plus export of search matches.

settings.title: Open from URL
settings.body: Fetch a document over HTTP(S) with None / Basic / Bearer / API-Key auth, remember credentials, and copy the request as cURL.

settings.title: Node statistics
settings.body: Right-click any container for child counts, a type histogram, distinct values and numeric min / max / avg — understand your data fast.

settings.feature: Memory tracks working set, not file size

settings.name: Free
settings.desc: Everything you need for everyday files.
settings.features:
JSON up to 100 MB
XML & CSV / TSV, any size
Tree view, find & filter

Export to JSON / XML / CSV

settings.desc: For engineers who live in big data files.

settings.quote: Opened an 8 GB NDJSON log that crashed everything else. It was on screen before I finished my coffee.
settings.author: Priya S.

settings.quote: The JSONPath query bar plus regex find replaced three scripts I used to run to inspect API dumps.
settings.author: Marco V.

settings.quote: Follow-tail on a live JSON log is the feature I didn't know I needed. Memory just doesn't move.
settings.author: Dan R.

settings.question: How large a file can it really open?
settings.answer: Around 10 GB comfortably. Files too large to index eagerly fall back to on-demand (lazy) indexing, so documents near or beyond your available RAM still open.

settings.question: Which formats are supported?
settings.answer: JSON, JSON-Lines / NDJSON, XML, CSV and TSV. The parser is chosen from the file extension, and you can also paste from the clipboard or open from a URL.

settings.question: Does my data leave my machine?
settings.answer: No. Files are memory-mapped and processed locally. Nothing is uploaded — the only network call is the optional Open-URL fetch you trigger yourself.

settings.question: What platforms does it run on?
settings.answer: Windows, macOS (universal, Apple Silicon + Intel) and Linux.

settings.question: What's the difference between Free and paid editions?
settings.answer: Free opens JSON up to 100 MB (XML/CSV any size). Premium and the Netcore Unbxd Edition remove the size limit and add automatic update checks; Enterprise adds volume licensing and managed deployment.

settings.heading: Stop waiting on progress bars.
settings.subheading: Download OPENXMLJSON free and open your biggest file in seconds.
settings.meta: Windows · macOS · Linux · Free edition available
